pv.title: Design Pattern Visualizer
simple-factory.name: Simple Factory
simple-factory.modes.transport.label: Transport
simple-factory.modes.transport.desc: A TransportFactory creates delivery vehicles based on a type parameter. The client requests a Transport object without knowing whether it gets a Truck, Ship, or Plane. The factory encapsulates all instantiation logic behind a single create() method.
simple-factory.details.transport.princip: Encapsulate object creation: the client never uses the new keyword for concrete products
Program to an interface (Transport), not an implementation (Truck, Ship, Plane)
Single Responsibility: factory owns all instantiation logic in one place
Easy to extend: adding a new transport type only requires modifying the factory class
Loose coupling: client depends on the abstraction, not on concrete classes

simple-factory.details.transport.concept.definition: A class with a single creation method that returns different types of objects based on a parameter. Not a GoF pattern per se, but a widely used idiom that simplifies object creation.
The common type (Transport) that all concrete products implement. The client codes against this interface, enabling polymorphism.
The factory method accepts a type string or enum and uses conditional logic (switch/if) to decide which concrete class to instantiate.
A specific implementation (Truck, Ship, Plane) of the product interface. The factory is the only place that references these classes directly.

simple-factory.details.transport.tradeof: Centralizes creation logic — one place to add or change product types
Client code stays clean and decoupled from concrete classes
Easy to understand — simpler than Factory Method or Abstract Factory
Reduces duplication of instantiation code across the codebase
Violates Open/Closed Principle — adding new types requires modifying the factory method
Factory can grow into a large switch/if block as product types increase
Single point of failure — all creation funnels through one class
Not as flexible as Factory Method pattern for extensibility via inheritance

Best when you have a small, stable set of product types, the creation logic is straightforward, and you want to hide instantiation details from the client without the overhead of a full Factory Method or Abstract Factory hierarchy.
static-factory.name: Static Factory
static-factory.modes.number.label: Number
static-factory.modes.number.desc: Number.of(value) inspects the input and returns an Integer, Float, or BigDecimal. Small integers are cached internally, so repeated calls for the same value return the same instance — a flyweight-like optimization that avoids redundant allocations.
static-factory.details.number.principles: Named constructors: Number.of(value) is more expressive than new Number(value) — the method name communicates intent
Caching and flyweight: small integers are cached internally, so of(42) called twice returns the same instance
Subtype selection: the factory inspects the input and returns Integer, Float, or BigDecimal without the caller knowing
Encapsulation of creation logic: the decision of which subclass to instantiate is hidden behind the static method
No public constructors: clients cannot bypass the factory, ensuring all instances go through the controlled creation path

static-factory.details.number.concepts.definition: A static method on a class that returns an instance of that class (or a subclass). Unlike constructors, static factory methods have names, can return cached instances, and can return subtypes.
Small integer values are cached in an internal Map. When of(42) is called again, the cached Integer(42) is returned instead of creating a new object — similar to the Flyweight pattern.
The factory inspects the runtime value to determine the concrete type: int values become Integer, floating-point become Float, and very large or precise values become BigDecimal.
A common naming convention (from Java) where valueOf() converts a primitive to its wrapper type. Number.of() delegates to valueOf() for integer inputs.

Descriptive naming: Number.of(42) and Number.zero() are self-documenting, unlike new Number(42)
Instance caching: frequently used values are shared, reducing memory allocation and GC pressure
Can return subtypes: of() returns Integer, Float, or BigDecimal — constructors cannot do this
Encapsulates creation logic: adding a new numeric type requires no client-side changes
Not discoverable via new keyword: developers must know the static method names exist
Cannot subclass a class that only provides static factory methods (no public constructor to call via super)
Cache management adds complexity: cache invalidation, memory leaks, and thread safety must be considered
Harder to identify in documentation: static methods blend in with other utility methods

Ideal for value types where instance caching benefits performance (small integers, enum-like values), when the return type should vary based on input, or when named construction methods improve API readability.
factory-method.name: Factory Method
factory-method.modes.logistics.label: Logistics
factory-method.modes.logistics.desc: Logistics app where each transport type has its own creator subclass implementing createTransport(). The parent Logistics class defines planDelivery() which calls the abstract createTransport() — each subclass decides which Transport to instantiate.
factory-method.details.logistics.princip: The Creator class declares a factory method that subclasses override to produce different Products
Client code works with the abstract Creator and Product types — never depends on concrete classes
Open/Closed Principle: new transport types are added by creating new creator subclasses without modifying existing code
Single Responsibility: each Concrete Creator is responsible for exactly one product type
planDelivery() in the Creator relies on the factory method — the Template Method pattern cooperates with Factory Method

factory-method.details.logistics.concept.definition: An abstract method declared in the Creator that returns a Product. Each Concrete Creator provides its own implementation.
Abstract class containing the factory method and business logic (planDelivery) that depends on the product returned by the factory method.
Subclass of Creator that overrides the factory method to return a specific Concrete Product (e.g., RoadLogistics returns Truck).
Interface or abstract class that declares the operations all concrete products must implement (e.g., deliver()).

factory-method.details.logistics.tradeof: Eliminates tight coupling between Creator and Concrete Products
Open/Closed Principle — extend without modifying existing code
Single Responsibility — each creator handles one product type
Supports the Dependency Inversion Principle — high-level code depends on abstractions
Requires a parallel class hierarchy (creator per product), increasing class count
More indirection than direct construction — harder to trace which object is created
Overkill if the product family is small and unlikely to grow
Subclassing solely for object creation can feel heavyweight

Use when the exact type of object to create is determined by subclasses, when you want to provide a hook for extensions, or when a class delegates instantiation responsibility to one of several helper subclasses.
abstract-factory.name: Abstract Factory
abstract-factory.modes.furniture.label: Furniture
abstract-factory.modes.furniture.desc: Furniture store: each style (Modern, Victorian) creates matching Chair, Table, and Sofa.
abstract-factory.details.furniture.princ: Program to interfaces: client code depends only on FurnitureFactory, Chair, Table, and Sofa abstractions
Product families guarantee consistency: ModernFurnitureFactory always produces matching modern products
Open/Closed Principle: new furniture styles (e.g., ArtDeco) can be added without modifying existing code
Single Responsibility: each concrete factory encapsulates creation logic for one product family
Dependency Inversion: high-level client depends on abstractions, not concrete product classes

abstract-factory.details.furniture.conce.definition: A set of related products (Chair + Table + Sofa) designed to work together in a specific style.
Interface declaring creation methods for each product type, ensuring every family produces a full set.
Implements creation methods to produce products of a specific variant (Modern, Victorian).
Each factory guarantees all produced objects belong to the same family, preventing mismatched combinations.
Client receives a factory via constructor/config and never references concrete product classes directly.

abstract-factory.details.furniture.trade: Guarantees product family consistency — all objects from one factory match in style
Isolates client from concrete classes — easy to swap entire families
Follows Open/Closed Principle — adding new families requires no changes to existing code
Centralizes creation logic — each factory is a single source of truth for its variant
Adding a new product type (e.g., Lamp) requires changing all factory interfaces and implementations
Increases number of classes significantly — each family multiplies the class count
Can be over-engineered for simple scenarios with few product types
Factory interfaces become rigid — hard to evolve without breaking existing families
When your system must create families of related objects that must be used together, and you want to enforce consistency across product variants without coupling client code to concrete classes.
builder.name: Builder
builder.modes.house.label: House
builder.modes.house.desc: Step-by-step house construction: foundation, walls, roof, interior — Director orchestrates the sequence.
builder.details.house.principles: Separate the construction algorithm (Director) from the object representation (Builder)
The Director defines the order of construction steps; the Builder knows how to perform each step
Different builders produce different representations using the same construction process
The Product is assembled incrementally — each step adds one component
The client retrieves the finished product from the builder, not from the director

builder.details.house.concepts.definition: Orchestrates the construction sequence. Calls builder methods in a fixed order but is decoupled from concrete product details.
Interface declaring construction steps: buildFoundation(), buildWalls(), buildRoof(), buildInterior(). Each concrete builder implements these.
Concrete builder that constructs a wooden house with log foundation, timber walls, shingle roof, and rustic interior.
Concrete builder that constructs a stone house with concrete foundation, stone walls, tile roof, and modern interior.
Product object whose properties are set one by one during construction. Retrieved via getResult() after all steps complete.

builder.details.house.tradeoffs.pros: Same construction process creates different representations (wooden vs. stone house)
Construction code is isolated from representation — easy to add new builder types
Fine-grained control over the construction process step by step
Product is always in a valid state once construction completes
builder.details.house.tradeoffs.cons: Overall complexity increases — requires Director, Builder interface, concrete builders, and Product
Overkill for simple objects that can be constructed in a single step
The number of classes grows with each new product variant
Director adds unnecessary abstraction when the construction sequence is trivial or never varies
builder.details.house.tradeoffs.whenToUs: Use when construction involves multiple steps that must follow a specific order, when the same process should create different representations, or when you want to avoid a constructor with many parameters.
prototype.name: Prototype
prototype.modes.shapes.label: Shapes
prototype.modes.shapes.desc: Clone geometric shapes (Circle, Rectangle, Triangle) without depending on their concrete classes. The client calls clone() on an existing shape object, and a deep copy spawns alongside the original — fully independent and ready to mutate.
prototype.details.shapes.principles: Clone objects without coupling to their concrete classes — the client only knows the Prototype interface
Avoid costly initialization: cloning bypasses complex constructor logic and expensive resource setup
Program to an interface (Shape.clone()), not an implementation (new Circle(...))
Each prototype is self-contained: it knows how to copy itself, preserving encapsulation
Open/Closed Principle: add new shape types without modifying client cloning code

prototype.details.shapes.concepts.definition: Declares clone() method that all concrete prototypes must implement. The client calls clone() without knowing the concrete type.
clone() produces a fully independent object — changing the clone does not affect the original, and vice versa.
A class (Circle, Rectangle, Triangle) that implements clone() by copying all its internal state into a new instance.
An optional catalog of pre-built prototypes that clients can look up by key and clone on demand, avoiding direct class references.

prototype.details.shapes.tradeoffs.pros: Eliminates the need for complex class hierarchies of factories
Cloning complex objects is faster than creating them from scratch
Client code is decoupled from concrete classes entirely
Adding new prototypes requires no changes to existing code
prototype.details.shapes.tradeoffs.cons: Deep copying complex objects with circular references can be tricky
Each concrete prototype must implement its own clone() correctly
Cloned objects share no identity — may need careful tracking
Some languages make deep copy harder than others (e.g., shallow copy pitfalls)
prototype.details.shapes.tradeoffs.whenT: Best when creating objects is expensive (complex initialization, database reads, network calls) and you have a set of pre-configured objects that can serve as templates for new instances.
singleton.name: Singleton
singleton.modes.database.label: Database
singleton.modes.database.desc: A DatabaseConnection singleton ensures only one connection instance exists. Multiple clients call getInstance() — the first call creates the connection, subsequent calls return the same instance. All clients share a single connection object.
singleton.details.database.principles: Ensure a class has only one instance — the constructor is private, getInstance() is the sole entry point
Lazy initialization: the instance is created only when first requested, not at application startup
Thread safety: in concurrent environments, getInstance() must handle race conditions (double-checked locking, volatile, enum, or synchronized block)
Global state risk: while Singleton provides controlled access, the shared mutable state can lead to unexpected side effects across the system
Single Responsibility tension: the class manages both its business logic (query, connect) and its own lifecycle (instance creation and storage)

singleton.details.database.concepts.definition: The instance is not created until the first call to getInstance(), saving resources if the singleton is never used during the application lifecycle.
A thread-safety optimization: check if instance is null without synchronization first, then synchronize only for the creation block — avoids lock overhead on every call.
A pattern where multiple singleton classes register themselves in a central registry, allowing lookup by name or type — useful when multiple controlled-instance classes coexist.
The constructor is made private to prevent external instantiation. Only the static getInstance() method can create the object, enforcing the single-instance guarantee.

singleton.details.database.tradeoffs.pro: Guarantees exactly one instance — prevents resource conflicts (e.g., multiple DB connections competing for the same port)
Global access without global variables — cleaner than static fields scattered across code
Lazy initialization saves resources when the instance is not always needed
Easy to implement in most languages with minimal boilerplate
singleton.details.database.tradeoffs.con: Violates Single Responsibility Principle — the class manages its own lifecycle and business logic
Makes unit testing harder — difficult to mock or replace the singleton in test environments
Introduces hidden dependencies — any class can call getInstance() without declaring the dependency in its constructor or interface
Thread safety adds complexity and potential performance overhead in concurrent environments
singleton.details.database.tradeoffs.whe: Use when exactly one instance of a class is needed system-wide (e.g., configuration manager, logging service, hardware driver interface), and you want controlled, lazy access to that instance.
pool.name: Object Pool
pool.modes.connections.label: Database
pool.modes.connections.desc: A ConnectionPool manages a fixed set of reusable database connections. Clients call acquire() to get a connection and release() to return it. Connections move between "Available" and "In Use" pools without being created or destroyed each time.
pool.details.connections.principles: Reuse over recreation: acquiring a connection from the pool is orders of magnitude cheaper than opening a new TCP/database handshake
Bounded resources: maxSize caps the number of concurrent connections, preventing resource exhaustion on the database server
Lifecycle management: the pool handles connection creation, validation (isValid), and teardown — clients just acquire and release
Fairness: when all connections are in use, new acquire() calls block or fail fast, preventing unbounded wait times
Transparency: clients use a Connection object the same way whether it was freshly created or recycled from the pool

pool.details.connections.concepts.definition: A container that holds pre-created objects (connections) and lends them out on demand. Avoids the cost of creating and destroying objects for each use.
Eager: all connections created at startup. Lazy: connections created on first acquire() up to maxSize. Trade-off between startup time and first-request latency.
Upper bound on the number of objects in the pool. Prevents resource exhaustion and ensures the system operates within capacity.
Before returning a connection from the pool, isValid() verifies it is still alive. Stale connections are discarded and replaced.

pool.details.connections.tradeoffs.pros: Drastically reduces connection creation overhead — reuse amortizes the cost
Bounded resource usage prevents database server overload
Centralized lifecycle management simplifies error handling and cleanup
Clients are decoupled from connection creation logic
pool.details.connections.tradeoffs.cons: Pool size tuning requires load testing — too small causes contention, too large wastes resources
Leaked connections (not released) can exhaust the pool silently
Added complexity: timeout handling, eviction policies, health checks
Stale or broken connections must be detected and replaced
pool.details.connections.tradeoffs.whenT: Use when creating objects is expensive (database connections, network sockets, heavyweight resources) and the same objects can be safely reused across multiple clients.
simple-factory.tooltip.factory: Factory class that encapsulates object creation logic based on a type parameter
simple-factory.tooltip.transport: Common interface for all transport types returned by the factory
simple-factory.tooltip.truck: Concrete product: delivers cargo by road
simple-factory.tooltip.ship: Concrete product: delivers cargo by sea
simple-factory.tooltip.plane: Concrete product: delivers cargo by air
simple-factory.tooltip.obj-truck: Runtime instance of Truck created by factory
simple-factory.tooltip.obj-ship: Runtime instance of Ship created by factory
simple-factory.tooltip.obj-plane: Runtime instance of Plane created by factory
simple-factory.step.transport.2: Factory selects Truck class based on type
simple-factory.step.transport.6: Client calls deliver() on returned object

simple-factory.depRules.name: Factory
simple-factory.depRules.role: Creates objects without exposing creation logic to the client

simple-factory.depRules.name: Product (Interface)
simple-factory.depRules.role: Common interface for all objects the factory can create

simple-factory.depRules.name: Concrete Products
simple-factory.depRules.role: Specific implementations returned by the factory

simple-factory.depRules.name: Client
simple-factory.depRules.role: Uses the factory to get objects, unaware of concrete classes

static-factory.tooltip.number: Static factory class with named creation methods — inspects input type and delegates to the appropriate subclass constructor
static-factory.tooltip.cache: Internal cache storing previously created Integer instances keyed by their int value
static-factory.tooltip.integer: Concrete product for whole number values — instances may be cached for small values
static-factory.tooltip.float: Concrete product for floating-point values — never cached, always a new instance
static-factory.tooltip.bigdecimal: Concrete product for arbitrary-precision decimal values
static-factory.tooltip.obj-int1: First Integer(42) instance — created and cached
static-factory.tooltip.obj-int2: Second request for 42 — same cached instance returned
static-factory.tooltip.obj-float1: Float instance — created fresh, not cached
static-factory.step.number.5: Return Integer(42) as Number to client
static-factory.step.number.8: Return cached Integer(42) — same instance, no allocation
static-factory.step.number.10: float detected — no caching for floats
static-factory.step.number.12: Return Float(3.14) as Number to client
static-factory.stepLabel.number: Create numbers via static methods with caching

static-factory.depRules.name: Static Factory
static-factory.depRules.role: Provides named static methods to create instances, improving readability over constructors

static-factory.depRules.name: Product
static-factory.depRules.role: Objects created and optionally cached by the static factory methods

static-factory.depRules.name: Cache
static-factory.depRules.role: Internal storage enabling instance reuse for equivalent inputs

factory-method.tooltip.logistics: Abstract creator that declares the factory method createTransport() and uses it inside planDelivery()
factory-method.tooltip.transport: Product interface defining the contract all concrete transports must implement
factory-method.tooltip.sea-logistics: Concrete creator that overrides createTransport() to return a Ship instance
factory-method.tooltip.road-logistics: Concrete creator that overrides createTransport() to return a Truck instance
factory-method.tooltip.obj-ship: Runtime Ship instance created by SeaLogistics
factory-method.tooltip.obj-truck: Runtime Truck instance created by RoadLogistics
factory-method.tooltip.truck: Concrete product implementing Transport — delivers goods by road
factory-method.tooltip.ship: Concrete product implementing Transport — delivers goods by sea
factory-method.step.logistics.0: Client invokes planDelivery() on the abstract Logistics reference
factory-method.step.logistics.1: planDelivery() internally calls the abstract createTransport() factory method
factory-method.step.logistics.2: Concrete creator overrides createTransport() to instantiate a Truck
factory-method.step.logistics.3: Truck constructor is invoked by RoadLogistics
factory-method.step.logistics.4: Truck instance is returned to planDelivery()
factory-method.step.logistics.5: planDelivery() calls deliver() on the returned Transport reference
factory-method.step.logistics.6: Concrete Truck executes delivery by road
factory-method.step.logistics.7: planDelivery() finishes — goods delivered by Truck

factory-method.depRules.name: Creator (Abstract)
factory-method.depRules.role: Declares the factory method returning a Product; may provide default implementation

factory-method.depRules.name: Concrete Creator
factory-method.depRules.role: Overrides factory method to return specific Product type

factory-method.depRules.name: Product (Interface)
factory-method.depRules.role: Common interface for all objects the factory method creates

abstract-factory.tooltip.factory: Abstract Factory: declares creation methods for each product in the family
abstract-factory.tooltip.chair: Abstract Product: interface for chair objects
abstract-factory.tooltip.table: Abstract Product: interface for table objects
abstract-factory.tooltip.sofa: Abstract Product: interface for sofa objects
abstract-factory.step.furniture.1: Modern factory selected for this order
abstract-factory.step.furniture.8: All modern products delivered to client

abstract-factory.depRules.name: Abstract Factory
abstract-factory.depRules.role: Declares creation methods for each product in the family

abstract-factory.depRules.name: Concrete Factory
abstract-factory.depRules.role: Implements creation methods for a specific product family/variant

abstract-factory.depRules.name: Abstract Product
abstract-factory.depRules.role: Interface for a type of product

abstract-factory.depRules.name: Concrete Product
abstract-factory.depRules.role: Implements abstract product for a specific variant

abstract-factory.depRules.name: Client
abstract-factory.depRules.role: Uses factories and products through abstract interfaces only

builder.tooltip.director: Orchestrates the construction sequence by calling builder methods in order
builder.tooltip.house-builder: Abstract builder interface declaring all construction steps
builder.tooltip.house: Product assembled step by step — each property is set during construction
builder.tooltip.wooden-builder: Builds a wooden house with log foundation, timber walls, shingle roof
builder.tooltip.stone-builder: Builds a stone house with concrete foundation, stone walls, tile roof
builder.step.house.0: Director begins construction by calling builder methods in order
builder.step.house.1: Builder lays the foundation — log pilings for wooden house
builder.step.house.3: Builder erects walls — timber frame with insulation
builder.step.house.7: Builder finishes interior — rustic wood paneling
builder.step.house.9: Builder returns the fully constructed House to Director
builder.step.house.10: Fully assembled House returned to the client
builder.stepLabel.house: Build a wooden house step by step

builder.depRules.name: Director
builder.depRules.role: Constructs an object using the Builder interface in a specific order

builder.depRules.name: Concrete Builder
builder.depRules.role: Implements construction steps and tracks the product being built

builder.depRules.name: Product
builder.depRules.role: The complex object being constructed step by step

prototype.tooltip.shape: Prototype interface declaring clone() — all concrete shapes implement this to produce copies of themselves
prototype.tooltip.circle: Concrete prototype that can clone itself, producing a deep copy with the same radius and color
prototype.tooltip.rectangle: Concrete prototype that clones itself with all dimension and color data
prototype.tooltip.triangle: Concrete prototype that produces an independent copy via clone()
prototype.tooltip.obj-circle-orig: Original Circle prototype instance — the source for cloning
prototype.tooltip.obj-circle-clone: Deep copy of Circle — independent from the original
prototype.tooltip.obj-rect-clone: Deep copy of Rectangle — independent from the original
prototype.step.shapes.0: Client has a reference to the original Circle object
prototype.step.shapes.1: Client calls clone() through the Prototype interface
prototype.step.shapes.2: Circle implements clone() — copies radius and color into new instance
prototype.step.shapes.3: Deep copy of Circle created — independent from original
prototype.step.shapes.4: Modifying clone.color does not affect original
prototype.step.shapes.5: Client now needs a copy of Rectangle
prototype.step.shapes.6: Client calls clone() on Rectangle through Prototype interface
prototype.step.shapes.7: Rectangle copies width, height, and color into new instance
prototype.step.shapes.8: Deep copy of Rectangle created — fully independent
prototype.step.shapes.9: Both clones exist independently — pattern complete

prototype.depRules.name: Concrete Prototype
prototype.depRules.role: Implements clone() creating a copy of itself

prototype.depRules.name: Client
prototype.depRules.role: Creates new objects by asking prototypes to clone themselves

singleton.tooltip.client1: First client — calls getInstance() which triggers instance creation
singleton.tooltip.client2: Second client — calls getInstance() and receives the same instance
singleton.tooltip.client3: Third client — calls getInstance() and receives the same instance again
singleton.tooltip.singleton: Singleton class — stores its own unique instance in a static field and provides global access via getInstance()
singleton.tooltip.obj-instance: The single shared instance — created on first call to getInstance()
singleton.step.database.0: Client 1 needs a database connection — calls DatabaseConnection.getInstance()
singleton.step.database.2: instance == null? Yes — first call, must create new instance
singleton.step.database.3: new DatabaseConnection() — private constructor called, singleton instance stored in static field
singleton.step.database.4: Client 1 receives the singleton DatabaseConnection instance
singleton.step.database.5: Client 2 needs a database connection — calls DatabaseConnection.getInstance()
singleton.step.database.6: Singleton checks static instance field again
singleton.step.database.7: instance == null? No — instance already exists, reuse it
singleton.step.database.8: Client 2 receives the exact same DatabaseConnection object — no new instance created
singleton.step.database.9: Client 3 needs a database connection — calls DatabaseConnection.getInstance()
singleton.step.database.10: Singleton checks static instance field once more
singleton.step.database.11: instance == null? No — still the same instance
singleton.step.database.12: Client 3 receives the same DatabaseConnection — all three clients share one instance
singleton.stepLabel.database: Three clients share one DB connection

singleton.depRules.name: Singleton
singleton.depRules.role: Stores its own unique instance; provides global access via static getInstance()

singleton.depRules.name: Client
singleton.depRules.role: Accesses the singleton exclusively through getInstance(), never via constructor

pool.tooltip.pool: Manages a fixed-size pool of reusable Connection objects — acquire() lends one out, release() takes it back
pool.tooltip.idle-conn: Idle connection ready to be acquired by a client
pool.tooltip.active-conn: Connection currently executing a query for a client
pool.tooltip.connection: A reusable database connection object — created once, recycled many times through the pool
pool.step.connections.0: Client calls acquire() on the ConnectionPool
pool.step.connections.2: Conn #1 moves from Available to In Use
pool.step.connections.3: Connection #1 now active in the In Use pool
pool.step.connections.4: Client executes SQL query through Conn #1
pool.step.connections.6: Conn #1 returned to Available pool — ready for reuse
pool.step.connections.8: Conn #2 moves from Available to In Use
pool.step.connections.9: Connection #2 now active in the In Use pool

pool.depRules.name: Object Pool
pool.depRules.role: Manages a collection of reusable objects, handling acquire and release operations

pool.depRules.name: Reusable Object
pool.depRules.role: Expensive-to-create object that is pooled and recycled instead of destroyed

pool.depRules.name: Client
pool.depRules.role: Acquires objects from the pool, uses them, and releases them back when done

adapter.name: Adapter
adapter.modes.payment.label: Payment Gateway
adapter.modes.payment.desc: StripeAdapter adapts the StripeAPI to the PaymentProcessor interface. The client calls pay(amount) on the processor without knowing the underlying payment service. The adapter translates the call into the Stripe-specific createCharge() method.
adapter.details.payment.principles: Convert the interface of a class into another interface that clients expect
Single Responsibility: the adapter handles only interface translation, not business logic
Open/Closed Principle: new adapters can be added without changing existing client code
Program to an interface (PaymentProcessor), not an implementation (StripeAPI)
Composition over inheritance: the object adapter wraps the adaptee rather than extending it

adapter.details.payment.concepts.definition: The interface the client expects (PaymentProcessor). All interactions go through this abstraction, keeping the client decoupled from concrete services.
A class (StripeAdapter) that implements the Target interface and translates calls to the Adaptee. It bridges the gap between incompatible interfaces.
The existing class (StripeAPI) with an incompatible interface. It has the functionality the client needs but cannot be used directly.
Object adapter uses composition (wraps the adaptee); class adapter uses multiple inheritance. Object adapter is more flexible and preferred in most languages.

adapter.details.payment.tradeoffs.pros: Enables integration of incompatible interfaces without modifying existing code
Single Responsibility — interface conversion is isolated in the adapter class
Open/Closed Principle — new adapters added without changing client or adaptee
Promotes reuse of existing functionality through wrapping
adapter.details.payment.tradeoffs.cons: Adds an extra layer of indirection, increasing complexity
Multiple adapters for the same adaptee can lead to code duplication
Can hide important interface differences that should be addressed at design level
Performance overhead from delegation through the adapter layer
adapter.details.payment.tradeoffs.whenTo: Use when you need to integrate a class with an incompatible interface into your system, wrap legacy or third-party code behind a stable interface, or provide a unified API over multiple services with different interfaces.
adapter.tooltip.client: Client that uses PaymentProcessor interface to process payments
adapter.tooltip.target: Target interface defining the pay(amount) contract the client expects
adapter.tooltip.adapter: Adapter converting PaymentProcessor calls to StripeAPI calls
adapter.tooltip.adaptee: Existing Stripe API with incompatible createCharge() interface
adapter.tooltip.obj-adaptee: Runtime instance of StripeAPI used by the adapter
adapter.step.payment.1: Polymorphic call: PaymentProcessor resolves to StripeAdapter

adapter.depRules.name: Target (PaymentProcessor)
adapter.depRules.role: Defines the interface the client expects

adapter.depRules.name: Adapter (StripeAdapter)
adapter.depRules.role: Converts the Target interface to the Adaptee interface

adapter.depRules.name: Adaptee (StripeAPI)
adapter.depRules.role: Existing class with an incompatible interface

adapter.depRules.name: Client
adapter.depRules.role: Uses the Target interface to interact with the Adaptee through the Adapter

facade.name: Facade
facade.modes.theater.label: Home Theater
facade.modes.theater.desc: HomeTheaterFacade simplifies managing a complex home theater system. Instead of calling methods on DVDPlayer, Amplifier, Projector, and Lights individually, the client calls watchMovie() on the facade, which orchestrates all subsystems in the correct order.
facade.details.theater.principles: Provide a unified, simplified interface to a set of interfaces in a subsystem
Minimize communication and dependencies between subsystems and clients
The facade delegates work to subsystem objects — it does not replace them
Clients can still access subsystem classes directly if they need fine-grained control
Principle of Least Knowledge (Law of Demeter): the client only talks to the facade

facade.details.theater.concepts.definition: A class (HomeTheaterFacade) providing a simple interface to a complex subsystem. It knows which subsystem classes to delegate to.
Complex classes (DVDPlayer, Amplifier, Projector, Lights) that do the actual work. They are unaware of the facade.
The facade exposes high-level operations (watchMovie, endMovie) that orchestrate multiple subsystem calls in the correct order.
The client depends only on the facade, not on subsystem internals. Subsystem changes are absorbed by the facade.

facade.details.theater.tradeoffs.pros: Simplifies complex subsystem usage with a clean, high-level API
Decouples clients from subsystem implementation details
Reduces the number of objects clients need to interact with
Promotes layered architecture and Principle of Least Knowledge
facade.details.theater.tradeoffs.cons: Can become a god object if too many responsibilities are added
May limit access to advanced subsystem features
Adds an extra layer of abstraction that may not always be needed
Changes in subsystem interfaces still require facade updates
facade.details.theater.tradeoffs.whenToU: Use when you want to provide a simple interface to a complex subsystem, when there are many dependencies between clients and the implementation classes, or when you want to layer your subsystems.
facade.tooltip.client: Client that uses the facade to control the home theater
facade.tooltip.facade: Facade providing watchMovie() and endMovie() high-level operations
facade.tooltip.amp: Amplifier subsystem — manages audio volume and power
facade.stepLabel.theater: Watch movie via Home Theater Facade

facade.depRules.name: Facade (HomeTheaterFacade)
facade.depRules.role: Provides a simplified interface to the subsystem

facade.depRules.name: Subsystem (DVDPlayer, Amplifier, Projector, Lights)
facade.depRules.role: Complex subsystem classes that do the actual work

facade.depRules.name: Client
facade.depRules.role: Uses the Facade instead of calling subsystem classes directly

proxy.name: Proxy
proxy.modes.caching.label: Database Caching
proxy.modes.caching.desc: CachingProxy intercepts SQL queries and caches results. On cache miss, it delegates to RealDatabase and stores the result. On cache hit, it returns the cached result without touching the database — improving performance for repeated queries.
proxy.details.caching.principles: Provide a surrogate or placeholder for another object to control access to it
The proxy and real subject implement the same interface — they are interchangeable
The proxy can add behavior (caching, access control, logging) without modifying the real subject
Lazy initialization: defer creation of expensive objects until they are actually needed
Transparent to the client — the client does not know whether it talks to a proxy or the real object

proxy.details.caching.concepts.definition: Delays creation of expensive objects until needed. Creates the real object on first access and caches it for subsequent use.
Controls access to the real object based on permissions. Checks credentials before delegating the request.
Stores results of expensive operations and returns cached data for identical requests, avoiding repeated computation or network calls.
Performs additional actions when the real object is accessed, such as reference counting, logging, or thread locking.

proxy.details.caching.tradeoffs.pros: Controls access without clients knowing about it
Caching proxy dramatically improves performance for repeated operations
Follows Open/Closed Principle — add behavior without modifying the real object
Can manage the lifecycle of the real service object
proxy.details.caching.tradeoffs.cons: Adds an extra layer of indirection, increasing complexity
Response time may increase due to proxy processing overhead
Cache invalidation is hard — stale data can cause subtle bugs
Proxy must be kept in sync with the real subject's interface
proxy.details.caching.tradeoffs.whenToUs: Use when you need lazy initialization (virtual proxy), access control (protection proxy), caching of expensive operations, logging, or when the real object is remote and you want a local representative.
proxy.tooltip.client: Client that sends SQL queries through the proxy
proxy.tooltip.subject: Subject interface declaring the query() contract
proxy.tooltip.proxy: CachingProxy that intercepts queries and caches results
proxy.tooltip.real: RealDatabase that executes actual SQL queries

proxy.depRules.name: Subject (DatabaseQuery)
proxy.depRules.role: Interface for both the real service and the proxy

proxy.depRules.name: RealSubject (RealDatabase)
proxy.depRules.role: The actual service that performs expensive operations

proxy.depRules.name: Proxy (CachingProxy)
proxy.depRules.role: Controls access to the RealSubject and adds caching behavior

proxy.depRules.name: Client
proxy.depRules.role: Works with the Subject interface, unaware of the proxy

decorator.name: Decorator
decorator.modes.coffee.label: Coffee Shop
decorator.modes.coffee.desc: MilkDecorator and SugarDecorator wrap a SimpleCoffee to add behavior dynamically. Each decorator implements the Beverage interface and delegates to the wrapped object, adding its own cost and description. Decorators can be stacked in any order.
decorator.details.coffee.principles: Attach additional responsibilities to an object dynamically — a flexible alternative to subclassing
Decorators implement the same interface as the component they wrap
Each decorator adds one responsibility and delegates the rest to the wrapped object
Open/Closed Principle: new behaviors added by creating new decorators, not modifying existing classes
Composition over inheritance: behavior is composed at runtime by stacking decorators

decorator.details.coffee.concepts.definition: Interface (Beverage) defining operations that can be dynamically extended. Both concrete components and decorators implement this.
Abstract class wrapping a Component reference. Forwards requests to the wrapped object and may add behavior before or after.
Each decorator holds a reference to the next component in the chain. Calling cost() propagates through the chain, accumulating results.
Decorators can wrap other decorators, forming a chain. The client interacts with the outermost decorator, unaware of the chain inside.

decorator.details.coffee.tradeoffs.pros: Add responsibilities dynamically without modifying existing code
Multiple decorators can be combined in any order for flexible behavior
Single Responsibility — each decorator handles one concern
Better alternative to a class explosion from subclassing every combination
decorator.details.coffee.tradeoffs.cons: Many small objects can make debugging harder
Order of wrapping matters — can lead to subtle bugs
Removing a specific decorator from the middle of a chain is complex
Initial design requires careful interface planning
decorator.details.coffee.tradeoffs.whenT: Use when you need to add responsibilities to objects dynamically and transparently, when extension by subclassing is impractical due to a large number of combinations, or when you want to add and remove behaviors at runtime.
decorator.tooltip.component: Beverage interface — defines cost() and description() for all beverages
decorator.tooltip.decorator: BeverageDecorator — abstract wrapper holding a Beverage reference
decorator.stepLabel.coffee: Build decorated coffee with Decorator pattern

decorator.depRules.name: Component (Beverage)
decorator.depRules.role: Interface for objects that can have responsibilities added dynamically

decorator.depRules.name: ConcreteComponent (SimpleCoffee)
decorator.depRules.role: The base object that can be decorated

decorator.depRules.name: Decorator (BeverageDecorator)
decorator.depRules.role: Abstract class wrapping a Component, forwarding requests

decorator.depRules.name: ConcreteDecorators (Milk, Sugar)
decorator.depRules.role: Add specific responsibilities to the component

bridge.name: Bridge
bridge.modes.remote.label: Remote Control
bridge.modes.remote.desc: RemoteControl (abstraction) is separated from Device (implementation). AdvancedRemote extends the abstraction, while TV and Radio provide concrete implementations. The bridge allows both hierarchies to vary independently.
bridge.details.remote.principles: Decouple an abstraction from its implementation so both can vary independently
Prefer composition over inheritance — the abstraction holds a reference to the implementation
Two orthogonal dimensions of change: abstraction hierarchy and implementation hierarchy
Clients work with the abstraction and are unaware of the implementation details
Open/Closed Principle: new abstractions and implementations can be added independently

bridge.details.remote.concepts.definition: The high-level control layer (RemoteControl) that delegates work to the implementation. It defines the control interface used by clients.
Interface (Device) defining low-level operations. Concrete implementors (TV, Radio) provide platform-specific behavior.
The composition relationship between Abstraction and Implementor. The abstraction holds a reference to the implementation, forming a bridge.
Two independent class hierarchies that can be extended without affecting each other: remotes (basic, advanced) and devices (TV, radio).

bridge.details.remote.tradeoffs.pros: Both hierarchies can be extended independently without modification
Implementation can be switched at runtime
Platform-specific code is isolated in implementor classes
Reduces class explosion from m×n combinations to m+n
bridge.details.remote.tradeoffs.cons: Increased complexity from the extra abstraction layer
Requires upfront identification of orthogonal dimensions
Overkill for simple scenarios with a single implementation
More indirection can make debugging harder
bridge.details.remote.tradeoffs.whenToUs: Use when you want to avoid a permanent binding between abstraction and implementation, when both abstraction and implementation should be extensible via subclassing, or when changes in implementation should not impact clients.
bridge.tooltip.abstraction: RemoteControl — holds a Device reference and delegates operations
bridge.tooltip.refined: AdvancedRemote — extends RemoteControl with mute() functionality
bridge.tooltip.impl: Device interface — defines low-level operations for all devices

bridge.depRules.name: Abstraction (RemoteControl)
bridge.depRules.role: Defines the control interface and holds a reference to a Device

bridge.depRules.name: RefinedAbstraction (AdvancedRemote)
bridge.depRules.role: Extends the abstraction with additional operations

bridge.depRules.name: ConcreteImplementor (TV, Radio)
bridge.depRules.role: Provide concrete implementations of the Device interface

composite.name: Composite
composite.modes.filesystem.label: File System
composite.modes.filesystem.desc: Directory and File both implement FileSystemItem. Directories can contain Files and sub-directories, forming a tree. getSize() is called recursively — leaves return their size, composites sum their children. The client treats individual files and directories uniformly.
composite.details.filesystem.principles: Compose objects into tree structures to represent part-whole hierarchies
Let clients treat individual objects and compositions uniformly
Recursive composition: composites contain components, which can themselves be composites
Single interface for both leaf and composite operations
Open/Closed Principle: new component types can be added without changing existing code

composite.details.filesystem.concepts.definition: Interface (FileSystemItem) declaring operations common to both leaf and composite objects. Enables uniform treatment.
A File object with no children. Implements operations directly (returns its own size).
A Directory that stores child components. Delegates operations to children and aggregates results (sums sizes).
Client calls getSize() on any FileSystemItem without knowing if it is a File or Directory. The composite pattern makes tree traversal transparent.

composite.details.filesystem.tradeoffs.p: Uniform treatment of individual objects and compositions
Easy to add new component types — just implement the Component interface
Simplifies client code that works with complex tree structures
Recursive operations are natural and clean
composite.details.filesystem.tradeoffs.c: Can make the design overly general — hard to restrict which components can be children
Type safety is reduced — client may try to add children to a leaf
Traversal of large trees can be expensive without optimization
Uniform interface may force leaf classes to implement irrelevant methods
composite.details.filesystem.tradeoffs.w: Use when you want to represent part-whole hierarchies of objects, when you want clients to treat individual objects and compositions uniformly, or when the structure can be represented as a tree.
composite.tooltip.component: FileSystemItem interface — getSize() and getName() for files and directories
composite.tooltip.leaf: File — leaf node with name and size, no children
composite.tooltip.composite: Directory — composite node containing children, delegates getSize()
composite.step.filesystem.cls0: Component interface defines getSize() + getName() contract
composite.step.filesystem.cls1: Composite: total=0, for each child: total += child.getSize()
composite.step.filesystem.cls2: Leaf: returns this.size directly, no children
composite.stepLabel.filesystem: Calculate file system size via Composite

composite.depRules.name: Component (FileSystemItem)
composite.depRules.role: Common interface for both leaves and composites

composite.depRules.name: Leaf (File)
composite.depRules.role: Has no children; implements operations directly

composite.depRules.name: Composite (Directory)
composite.depRules.role: Stores child components and delegates operations to them

composite.depRules.name: Client
composite.depRules.role: Works with all elements through the Component interface

flyweight.name: Flyweight
flyweight.modes.forest.label: Forest Rendering
flyweight.modes.forest.desc: TreeFactory shares TreeType objects (intrinsic state: name, color, texture) across many Tree instances (extrinsic state: x, y coordinates). When the factory receives a request for an existing type, it returns the cached object instead of creating a new one — drastically reducing memory usage.
flyweight.details.forest.principles: Share common state (intrinsic) across multiple objects to reduce memory consumption
Separate intrinsic state (shared, immutable) from extrinsic state (unique, context-dependent)
Use a factory to manage flyweight instances and ensure sharing
Flyweight objects must be immutable — their intrinsic state never changes after creation
Trade computation for memory: extrinsic state is recalculated or passed in rather than stored

flyweight.details.forest.concepts.definition: Shared data stored inside the flyweight (TreeType: name, color, texture). Immutable and context-independent — the same across all trees of this type.
Context-specific data stored outside the flyweight (Tree: x, y coordinates). Unique to each usage context and passed to the flyweight when needed.
Creates and caches flyweight objects (TreeFactory). Returns existing instances for repeated requests, ensuring maximum sharing.
Flyweight objects must be immutable to be safely shared. Any mutable state must be extrinsic and managed by the client or context.

flyweight.details.forest.tradeoffs.pros: Dramatically reduces memory usage when many objects share common state
Flyweight factory ensures deduplication of shared state
Immutable shared objects are inherently thread-safe
Separating intrinsic/extrinsic state clarifies the data model
flyweight.details.forest.tradeoffs.cons: Increased code complexity from splitting state into intrinsic and extrinsic
CPU overhead from recalculating or passing extrinsic state on each use
Debugging is harder when many objects reference the same flyweight
Not beneficial when objects have little shared state
flyweight.details.forest.tradeoffs.whenT: Use when an application creates a huge number of similar objects consuming significant memory, when most of the object state can be made extrinsic, and when the application does not depend on object identity.
flyweight.tooltip.client: Client that plants trees using the factory
flyweight.tooltip.factory: TreeFactory — caches and reuses TreeType flyweight objects
flyweight.tooltip.flyweight: TreeType — shared intrinsic state (name, color, texture)
flyweight.tooltip.obj-tree2: Tree at (50,70) reusing same Oak TreeType
flyweight.stepLabel.forest: Render forest with shared Flyweight objects

flyweight.depRules.name: Flyweight (TreeType)
flyweight.depRules.role: Stores intrinsic (shared) state that can be reused across contexts

flyweight.depRules.name: FlyweightFactory (TreeFactory)
flyweight.depRules.role: Creates and manages flyweight objects, ensuring sharing

flyweight.depRules.name: Context (Tree)
flyweight.depRules.role: Stores extrinsic (unique) state and references a flyweight

flyweight.depRules.name: Client
flyweight.depRules.role: Maintains references to flyweights and computes/stores extrinsic state

observer.name: Observer
observer.modes.events.label: Events
observer.modes.events.desc: An EventManager (subject) maintains a list of observers and notifies them automatically when an event occurs. EmailListener, LoggingListener, and SlackListener all implement the Observer interface and react independently to the same event.
observer.details.events.principles: Loose coupling: the subject knows only that observers implement an interface, not their concrete types
Open/Closed Principle: new observers can be added without modifying the subject
One-to-many dependency: one event triggers updates in many observers simultaneously
Event-driven design: components react to events rather than polling for changes
Single Responsibility: each observer handles its own reaction logic independently

observer.details.events.concepts.definition: The object being observed (EventManager). It maintains a subscriber list and provides subscribe/unsubscribe/notify methods.
An interface declaring the update(event, data) method. All concrete observers implement this interface to receive notifications.
A named occurrence (e.g., 'order.placed') that triggers notification. Observers subscribe to specific event types.
In push model, the subject sends data with the notification. In pull model, observers query the subject for data after being notified.

observer.details.events.tradeoffs.pros: Decouples event producers from consumers — they evolve independently
Easy to add new observers without touching existing code
Supports broadcast communication — one event reaches many listeners
Enables reactive architectures and event-driven systems
observer.details.events.tradeoffs.cons: Observers are notified in undefined order — can cause subtle bugs
Memory leaks if observers are not properly unsubscribed
Cascade updates can lead to performance issues with many observers
Debugging is harder — tracing event flow through multiple observers is complex
observer.details.events.tradeoffs.whenTo: Use when changes to one object require updating others, and you don't know how many objects need to change. Common in event systems, UI frameworks, message brokers, and pub/sub architectures.
observer.tooltip.subject: EventManager maintains a map of event names to observer lists and broadcasts notifications
observer.tooltip.observer: Observer interface — all concrete observers must implement update(event, data)
observer.tooltip.email: EmailListener sends confirmation emails when events occur
observer.tooltip.logging: LoggingListener writes event details to a log file
observer.tooltip.slack: SlackListener posts event notifications to a Slack channel
observer.tooltip.obj-email: Runtime instance of EmailListener processing the event
observer.tooltip.obj-logging: Runtime instance of LoggingListener writing to log
observer.tooltip.obj-slack: Runtime instance of SlackListener posting to Slack

observer.depRules.name: EventManager (Subject)
observer.depRules.role: Manages subscriber list and broadcasts events to all registered observers

observer.depRules.name: Observer (interface)
observer.depRules.role: Defines the update(event, data) contract for receiving notifications

observer.depRules.name: EmailListener
observer.depRules.role: Sends email notifications when events occur

observer.depRules.name: LoggingListener
observer.depRules.role: Logs event details to a file for auditing

observer.depRules.name: SlackListener
observer.depRules.role: Posts event notifications to a Slack channel

strategy.name: Strategy
strategy.modes.navigation.label: Navigation
strategy.modes.navigation.desc: A Navigator (context) holds a reference to a RouteStrategy and delegates route calculation to it. The client can swap strategies at runtime — DrivingStrategy, WalkingStrategy, or TransitStrategy — without changing the Navigator code.
strategy.details.navigation.principles: Encapsulate algorithms: each strategy encapsulates a specific route calculation algorithm
Open/Closed Principle: new strategies can be added without modifying the context
Composition over inheritance: the context delegates to a strategy object rather than using subclasses
Context delegation: the Navigator delegates route building to the current strategy
Runtime flexibility: strategies can be swapped at runtime to change behavior dynamically

strategy.details.navigation.concepts.definition: The Navigator class that maintains a reference to a strategy and delegates algorithm execution to it.
The RouteStrategy interface declaring the buildRoute() method. All concrete strategies implement this interface.
A specific algorithm implementation (DrivingStrategy, WalkingStrategy, TransitStrategy) that can be swapped at runtime.
Configures the context with a specific strategy and can change it at any time.

strategy.details.navigation.tradeoffs.pr: Eliminates conditional statements for algorithm selection
Algorithms can be swapped at runtime without modifying context code
Each strategy is independently testable and maintainable
New strategies can be added without touching existing code
strategy.details.navigation.tradeoffs.co: Clients must be aware of different strategies to select the appropriate one
Increases the number of classes in the system
Communication overhead between context and strategy for shared data
Overkill for a small, fixed set of algorithms that rarely change
strategy.details.navigation.tradeoffs.wh: Use when you have multiple algorithms for a specific task and want to switch between them at runtime, when you have conditional logic selecting different behaviors, or when you want to isolate algorithm implementation details from the code that uses them.
strategy.tooltip.client: Client configures the Navigator with a chosen strategy
strategy.tooltip.context: Navigator holds a strategy reference and delegates route building to it
strategy.tooltip.strategy: RouteStrategy interface — all concrete strategies must implement buildRoute()
strategy.tooltip.driving: DrivingStrategy calculates the fastest driving route
strategy.tooltip.transit: TransitStrategy calculates public transit route with transfers
strategy.tooltip.obj-driving: Runtime instance of DrivingStrategy with computed route
strategy.tooltip.obj-walking: Runtime instance of WalkingStrategy with computed route
strategy.tooltip.obj-transit: Runtime instance of TransitStrategy with computed route

strategy.depRules.name: Navigator (Context)
strategy.depRules.role: Maintains reference to current strategy and delegates route building

strategy.depRules.name: TransitStrategy
strategy.depRules.role: Calculates public transit route with transfers

strategy.depRules.name: Client
strategy.depRules.role: Configures Navigator with a chosen strategy

command.name: Command
command.modes.remote.label: Remote Control
command.modes.remote.desc: A RemoteControl (invoker) stores a Command object and executes it on button press. TurnOnCommand and TurnOffCommand encapsulate Light operations and support undo. The invoker is completely decoupled from the receiver.
command.details.remote.principles: Encapsulate requests as objects: each command wraps a specific action with its parameters
Decouple invoker from receiver: RemoteControl doesn't know about Light directly
Command history enables undo/redo: executed commands are stored for reversal
Single Responsibility: each command class handles one specific operation
Open/Closed Principle: new commands can be added without modifying invoker or receiver

command.details.remote.concepts.definition: An interface declaring execute() and undo() methods. Each concrete command encapsulates an action and its receiver.
The RemoteControl that stores and triggers commands without knowing what they do or who handles them.
The Light object that knows how to perform the actual work (turnOn, turnOff).
Commands stored in a history stack can be undone by calling their undo() method in reverse order.

command.details.remote.tradeoffs.pros: Decouples the object that invokes the operation from the one that performs it
Commands can be queued, logged, scheduled, and undone
Supports macro commands — composite commands executing multiple actions
Easy to add new commands without changing existing code
command.details.remote.tradeoffs.cons: Increases the number of classes — one class per command
Can be overkill for simple operations without undo requirements
Undo can be complex when commands have side effects on external systems
Command parameters must be serializable if commands are persisted
command.details.remote.tradeoffs.whenToU: Use when you want to parameterize objects with operations, queue or schedule operations for later execution, implement undo/redo functionality, or structure a system around high-level operations built from primitive ones.
command.tooltip.invoker: RemoteControl stores and executes commands, maintains undo history
command.tooltip.command: Command interface — declares execute() and undo() methods
command.tooltip.on: TurnOnCommand encapsulates Light.turnOn() and its undo
command.tooltip.off: TurnOffCommand encapsulates Light.turnOff() and its undo
command.tooltip.receiver: Light is the receiver that performs the actual on/off operations
command.tooltip.obj-on: Runtime instance of TurnOnCommand in the history stack
command.tooltip.obj-off: Runtime instance of TurnOffCommand after undo
command.tooltip.obj-light: Runtime instance of Light with current state
command.tooltip.client: Client creates receiver, commands, and wires them to the invoker
command.step.remote.0: Client creates Light, TurnOnCommand, and wires RemoteControl
command.stepLabel.remote: Execute and undo via Command pattern

command.depRules.name: RemoteControl (Invoker)
command.depRules.role: Stores and executes commands, maintains undo history

state.name: State
state.modes.order.label: Order Lifecycle
state.modes.order.desc: An Order (context) delegates behavior to its current OrderState. As the order progresses through Draft → Paid → Shipped → Delivered, each state determines which transitions are valid and handles them differently.
state.details.order.principles: State encapsulation: each state is a separate class with its own behavior
Eliminates conditionals: no switch/if chains — polymorphism handles state-specific logic
Open/Closed Principle: new states can be added without modifying existing ones
Single Responsibility: each state class handles only its own transitions
Explicit transitions: state changes are clear and traceable through setState() calls

state.details.order.concepts.definition: The Order class that maintains a reference to the current state and delegates behavior to it.
The OrderState interface declaring methods for all possible actions (pay, ship, deliver).
A specific state implementation (DraftState, PaidState, etc.) that handles transitions from that state.
A state change triggered by a method call. Each state determines which transitions are valid.

state.details.order.tradeoffs.pros: Organizes state-specific code into separate classes
Makes state transitions explicit and easy to trace
Eliminates complex conditional logic for state-dependent behavior
New states can be added without changing existing state classes
state.details.order.tradeoffs.cons: Can be overkill for objects with few simple states
Increases the number of classes in the system
State transitions can be hard to track when distributed across classes
Shared data between states requires careful design
state.details.order.tradeoffs.whenToUse: Use when an object's behavior depends on its state and changes at runtime, when operations have large conditional statements based on state, or when you want to model a finite state machine with clear transitions.
state.tooltip.context: Order maintains current state and delegates pay/ship/deliver calls to it
state.tooltip.state: OrderState interface — declares pay(), ship(), deliver() for all states
state.tooltip.draft: DraftState: initial state, only pay() is valid
state.tooltip.paid: PaidState: payment received, only ship() is valid
state.tooltip.shipped: ShippedState: in transit, only deliver() is valid
state.tooltip.delivered: DeliveredState: final state, no transitions allowed
state.tooltip.obj-draft: Runtime DraftState instance — initial order state
state.step.order.7: DeliveredState: order complete, no more transitions

state.depRules.name: Order (Context)
state.depRules.role: Maintains current state and delegates behavior

state.depRules.name: DraftState
state.depRules.role: Initial state, can transition to Paid

state.depRules.name: PaidState
state.depRules.role: Payment received, can transition to Shipped

state.depRules.name: ShippedState
state.depRules.role: In transit, can transition to Delivered

chain-of-responsibility.name: Chain of Responsibility
chain-of-responsibility.modes.middleware: An HTTP request passes through a chain of handlers: AuthHandler validates the token, ValidationHandler checks the request body, and ThrottleHandler enforces rate limits. Each handler either processes the request or passes it to the next handler in the chain.
chain-of-responsibility.details.middlewa: Decouple sender from receiver: the client doesn't know which handler will process the request
Chain of handlers: each handler decides to process the request or pass it along
Single Responsibility: each handler focuses on one specific concern
Open/Closed Principle: new handlers can be added to the chain without modifying existing ones
Flexible ordering: handlers can be rearranged, added, or removed at runtime

chain-of-responsibility.details.middlewa.definition: An interface declaring handle(request) and setNext(handler). Each handler either processes the request or delegates to the next.
A specific handler (AuthHandler, ValidationHandler, ThrottleHandler) that implements its own check logic.
A linked list of handlers where each handler holds a reference to the next. The request flows through until handled.
Assembles the chain and sends requests to the first handler. Doesn't know which handler will ultimately process the request.

chain-of-responsibility.details.middlewa: Decouples request senders from receivers — promotes loose coupling
Handlers can be dynamically added, removed, or reordered
Each handler has a single responsibility — easy to test and maintain
Follows Open/Closed Principle — new handlers don't affect existing ones
No guarantee that the request will be handled — it can fall through the chain
Debugging can be difficult when tracking which handler processed a request
Performance overhead if the chain is long and most handlers just pass through
Circular references in the chain can cause infinite loops

Use when multiple objects can handle a request and the handler isn't known in advance, when you want to issue a request to one of several objects without specifying the receiver explicitly, or in middleware pipelines, event processing, and logging frameworks.
chain-of-responsibility.tooltip.client: Client sends HTTP request to the handler chain
chain-of-responsibility.tooltip.handler: Handler interface — declares handle() and setNext() methods
chain-of-responsibility.tooltip.auth: AuthHandler validates JWT tokens on incoming requests
chain-of-responsibility.tooltip.valid: ValidationHandler validates request body against schema
chain-of-responsibility.tooltip.throttle: ThrottleHandler enforces rate limiting per client IP
chain-of-responsibility.tooltip.obj-vali: ValidationHandler passed — request body is valid
chain-of-responsibility.tooltip.obj-thro: ThrottleHandler passed — rate limit not exceeded
chain-of-responsibility.tooltip.obj-resp: All handlers passed — 200 OK response returned
Token valid → pass to next handler
Validation passed → pass to next handler
chain-of-responsibility.stepLabel.middle: Process request via Chain of Responsibility

chain-of-responsibility.depRules.name: Client
chain-of-responsibility.depRules.role: Sends request through the handler chain

iterator.name: Iterator
iterator.modes.playlist.label: Playlist
iterator.modes.playlist.desc: A Playlist (aggregate) creates a PlaylistIterator that traverses its Song collection sequentially. The client uses hasNext()/next() to iterate without knowing the internal storage structure of the playlist.
iterator.details.playlist.principles: Encapsulate traversal: the iterator hides the internal structure of the collection
Single Responsibility: the collection manages storage, the iterator manages traversal
Uniform interface: all iterators share hasNext()/next() regardless of the underlying collection
Open/Closed Principle: new iterator types can be added without modifying the collection
Multiple traversals: different iterators can traverse the same collection simultaneously

iterator.details.playlist.concepts.definition: The Playlist collection that stores songs and provides a createIterator() factory method.
An interface declaring hasNext() and next() methods for sequential access to elements.
PlaylistIterator that maintains traversal state (current index) and knows how to access Playlist elements.
A Song object stored in the collection, accessed sequentially through the iterator.

iterator.details.playlist.tradeoffs.pros: Hides the internal structure of collections from clients
Supports multiple simultaneous traversals of the same collection
Provides a uniform interface for traversing different collection types
Simplifies the collection interface by extracting traversal into its own class
iterator.details.playlist.tradeoffs.cons: Overkill for simple collections that are easily traversed with a loop
Can be less efficient than direct access for random-access collections
Iterator state can become stale if the collection is modified during iteration
Adds extra classes for each collection type
iterator.details.playlist.tradeoffs.when: Use when you want to access a collection's elements without exposing its internal structure, when you need to support multiple traversal algorithms over a collection, or when you want a uniform interface for traversing different collection types.
iterator.tooltip.playlist: Playlist stores songs and creates iterators for traversal
iterator.tooltip.iterator: Iterator interface — declares hasNext() and next() methods
iterator.tooltip.bidir: Extends Iterator with reverse traversal — hasPrevious() and previous()
iterator.tooltip.concrete: Bidirectional PlaylistIterator — traverses forward and backward
iterator.step.playlist.8: Traversal complete — forward and backward navigation demonstrated

iterator.depRules.name: Playlist (Aggregate)
iterator.depRules.role: Collection of songs that creates iterators

iterator.depRules.name: Iterator (interface)
iterator.depRules.role: Defines hasNext() and next() traversal contract

mediator.name: Mediator
mediator.modes.chatroom.label: Chat Room
mediator.modes.chatroom.desc: A ChatRoom (mediator) coordinates communication between User objects. Instead of users referencing each other directly, they send messages through the ChatRoom, which broadcasts to all participants except the sender.
mediator.details.chatroom.principles: Centralized communication: all inter-object communication goes through the mediator
Loose coupling: colleagues know only the mediator interface, not each other
Single Responsibility: the mediator handles coordination, colleagues handle their own logic
Simplified protocols: many-to-many relationships become one-to-many via the mediator
Open/Closed Principle: new colleague types can be added without changing existing ones

mediator.details.chatroom.concepts.definition: An interface declaring sendMessage() for routing messages between colleagues.
ChatRoom implements the mediator interface and knows all registered users to route messages.
User objects that communicate through the mediator rather than directly with each other.
Data sent from one colleague to others, routed through the mediator.

mediator.details.chatroom.tradeoffs.pros: Reduces coupling — components communicate through a single mediator
Centralizes complex communication logic in one place
Components can be reused independently of the mediator
Simplifies object protocols from many-to-many to one-to-many
mediator.details.chatroom.tradeoffs.cons: Mediator can become a God Object if too much logic accumulates
Single point of failure — if the mediator fails, all communication stops
Can be harder to understand the flow when all communication is indirect
Performance bottleneck if all messages route through one object
mediator.details.chatroom.tradeoffs.when: Use when a set of objects communicate in complex but well-defined ways, when reusing an object is difficult because it references many other objects, or when you want to customize behavior distributed between several classes without creating many subclasses.
mediator.tooltip.mediator: Mediator interface — declares sendMessage() for routing messages
mediator.tooltip.chatroom: ChatRoom manages users and broadcasts messages to all except sender
mediator.tooltip.user: User communicates through the mediator, not directly with other users
mediator.tooltip.obj-alice: Alice — a user in the chat room
mediator.tooltip.obj-bob: Bob — a user in the chat room
mediator.tooltip.obj-charlie: Charlie — a user in the chat room
mediator.step.chatroom.5: ChatRoom broadcasts to all except sender
mediator.stepLabel.chatroom: Chat room communication via Mediator pattern

mediator.depRules.name: ChatRoom
mediator.depRules.role: Concrete mediator that routes messages between users

mediator.depRules.name: User
mediator.depRules.role: Colleague that communicates via the mediator

memento.name: Memento
memento.modes.editor.label: Text Editor
memento.modes.editor.desc: An Editor (originator) creates EditorState snapshots (mementos) that are stored in a History (caretaker). The editor can type text, save snapshots, and restore previous states for undo functionality.
memento.details.editor.principles: Encapsulation preserved: the memento stores state without exposing the originator's internals
Single Responsibility: the originator manages state, the caretaker manages history
Immutable snapshots: memento objects are immutable once created, ensuring integrity
Separation of concerns: undo logic is in the caretaker, not the originator
State externalization: the originator's state is externalized without violating encapsulation

memento.details.editor.concepts.definition: The Editor that creates mementos containing snapshots of its current state and can restore from them.
EditorState — an immutable snapshot of the editor's content at a specific point in time.
History — stores and manages a stack of mementos without examining or modifying their contents.
A frozen copy of the originator's state that can be used to restore the originator later.

memento.details.editor.tradeoffs.pros: Provides undo/redo without exposing implementation details
Preserves encapsulation — only the originator accesses its own state
Simplifies the originator by offloading history management to the caretaker
Snapshots can be serialized for persistence or network transfer
memento.details.editor.tradeoffs.cons: Can consume significant memory if mementos are large or created frequently
Caretaker must manage memento lifecycle to avoid memory leaks
In some languages, ensuring memento immutability requires extra effort
Frequent snapshots of complex objects can impact performance
memento.details.editor.tradeoffs.whenToU: Use when you need to provide undo/redo functionality, when you need to save and restore object state without violating encapsulation, or when direct access to an object's state would expose implementation details.
memento.tooltip.editor: Editor (originator) creates and restores from state snapshots
memento.tooltip.memento: EditorState stores an immutable snapshot of editor content
memento.tooltip.history: History (caretaker) maintains a stack of saved states
memento.stepLabel.editor: Save and restore via Memento pattern

memento.depRules.name: Editor (Originator)
memento.depRules.role: Creates and restores from state snapshots

memento.depRules.name: History (Caretaker)
memento.depRules.role: Stores and manages the memento stack

template-method.name: Template Method
template-method.modes.mining.label: Data Mining
template-method.modes.mining.desc: A DataMiner abstract class defines a mine() template method with a fixed algorithm skeleton: openFile → extractData → parseData → analyze → sendReport. Subclasses (CSVMiner, JSONMiner, XMLMiner) override the variable steps while the invariant steps remain in the base class.
template-method.details.mining.principle: Define algorithm skeleton: the template method outlines the steps, subclasses fill in the details
Hollywood Principle: 'Don't call us, we'll call you' — the base class calls subclass methods
Code reuse: common steps (analyze, sendReport) live in the base class, not duplicated
Open/Closed Principle: new miners can be added by subclassing without changing the template
Inversion of Control: the base class controls the algorithm flow, subclasses customize steps

template-method.details.mining.concepts.definition: The mine() method that defines the algorithm skeleton. It calls abstract and concrete methods in a specific order.
DataMiner declares the template method and abstract steps (openFile, extractData, parseData) that subclasses must implement.
CSVMiner, JSONMiner, XMLMiner — each overrides the abstract steps to handle their specific file format.
Optional methods with default implementation that subclasses can override for additional customization points.

template-method.details.mining.tradeoffs: Eliminates code duplication — common logic stays in the base class
Provides a clear algorithm structure that's easy to understand
Subclasses customize only the parts that vary — less code to write
Enforces consistent algorithm structure across all variants
Requires inheritance — limits flexibility compared to composition-based patterns
More steps in the template mean more methods to override
Violates Liskov Substitution if subclasses suppress base class steps
Can be hard to maintain when the template has many optional hook methods

Use when you have several classes with similar algorithms that differ only in certain steps, when you want to let subclasses extend specific steps of an algorithm without changing its structure, or when you want to control the extension points available to subclasses.
template-method.tooltip.abstract: DataMiner defines mine() template with abstract steps for subclasses to override
template-method.tooltip.csv: CSVMiner overrides openFile/extractData/parseData for CSV files
template-method.tooltip.json: JSONMiner overrides openFile/extractData/parseData for JSON files
template-method.tooltip.xml: XMLMiner overrides openFile/extractData/parseData for XML files
